Population: White adult women who are Women United donors and committee members.
Summary Keywords:
Population: White adult women, Women United donors & committee members
Community Strengths – What’s Valued
Strong neighborhood connection and familiarity (Wells, South Portland, Portland, Kennebunks).
Long-term residency creating deep roots and safety.
Supportive neighbors (childcare help, meals, advice, shared resources).
Passionate, civically engaged communities willing to speak up.
Small-city accessibility — “one degree of separation” advantage.
Cross-boundary personal communities (not just geographic neighborhoods).
Engaged school communities, despite cultural and language differences.
Challenges – Barriers to Thriving
Childcare access & affordability
Waitlists, limited aftercare, misalignment with work schedules.
Expensive and inflexible programs.
Healthcare access (especially behavioral health)
Long waitlists; complex, fragmented systems.
Families responsible for care coordination.
Institutional strain and burnout among providers.
Affordable housing shortages
Scarcity mindset
Limited resources create tension and competition.
Siloed systems
Schools, employers, healthcare, and families operate separately.
Communication gaps
Loss of trusted local information sources (e.g., print newspapers).
Overreliance on fragmented or divisive social media channels.
Aspirations – Desired Community
Reliable access to childcare, healthcare, housing.
A proactive, “whole family” support approach.
Trust built through availability and consistency.
Empathy, kindness, and leading with good intentions.
Safe environments where children and families feel secure.
Stronger collaboration across institutions.
“People helping people” beyond individual family units.
Action Ideas – What Could Help
A trusted, locally grounded “North Star” resource hub.
Not necessarily a new building — could leverage libraries, schools, town halls.
Centralized, accessible information on how to get and give help.
Reimagining connection spaces once filled by churches.
Creating intentional cross-community dialogue spaces (“bubble popping”).
Localizing resources like 211 to feel more personal and community-based.
Strengthening partnerships between schools, healthcare providers, and community organizations.
More collaboration and less fragmentation across systems.
Overarching Themes
Connection vs. isolation.
Scarcity vs. shared abundance.
Trust built through visibility, access, and empathy.
Desire for stronger community infrastructure — relational, not just transactional.

Strengthen relational guidance and individualized support.
Build systems that promote belonging and preparation for adulthood.

Name of Organization/Group: SoPo Unite, High School Students, Afternoon Session
Population: Youth
Summary Keywords:
South Portland, community, transportation, Boys and Girls Club, volunteer opportunities, safety concerns, funding issues, student advocacy, community events, high school sports, student loans, summer activities, communication, resource allocation, student engagement
Core Themes:
Community Strengths – What’s Working
Proximity & accessibility
Many destinations close by (Boys & Girls Club, Wainwright Field, mall, elementary schools).
Walkable/bikeable for many students.
Boys & Girls Club as critical support
Safe, reliable space for younger siblings.
Especially important for single-parent families.
Strong school-based communities
Clubs, sports, track team, Black Student Union, SoPo Unite, PATHS.
Students feel there is “always someone to help.”
Abundance of resources
Mental health supports, addiction help, family resources regularly communicated.
Student leadership opportunities
Advocacy groups and informational parent events.
Diverse opportunities
Volunteer options, extracurriculars, technical programs (PATHS).
Key Challenges – What Makes Life Harder
Transportation barriers
Metro bus is slow, inconsistent, hard to navigate.
Long commute times (30–60+ minutes for short distances).
Especially difficult for middle schoolers and newcomers.
Safety concerns
Fear related to broader world events.
Concerns about ICE presence in Red Bank.
Girls’ personal safety at night.
Lack of coordination across systems
City/health departments not communicating well.
Families juggling overlapping requirements.
Funding inequities
Girls’ sports receive significantly less funding than boys’ sports.
High equipment costs (e.g., lacrosse).
New facilities built while some programs lack basic resources.
Cost barriers
Game admissions, prom costs, participation fees limit access.
Declining student engagement
Lower participation in community events.
Social divisions among students.
Communication gaps
Overreliance on email; announcements not always effectively shared.
Limited summer options
Few structured activities for teens in the “in-between” age (not camp, not full-time work).
College & career preparedness
Insufficient education about student loans and postsecondary options.
PATHS highly competitive and siloed.
Need more exposure to non-college pathways.
Aspirations – What Students Want
Faster, more reliable transportation.
More equitable funding across sports and activities.
Reduced cost barriers for school events.
Greater recognition for girls’ athletics and non-athletic achievements.
More inclusive, less divided school culture.
Expanded college and career education (including alternatives to college).
More free, accessible summer and community activities.
Stronger student voice and advocacy participation.
